elements.sent-2411-001: Given a compact K\"ahler manifold $(X,\omega)$, due to the work of Darvas--Di Nezza--Lu, the space of singularity types of $\omega$-psh functions admits a natural pseudo-metric $d_\mathcal S$ that is complete in the presence of positive mass.
elements.sent-2411-002: When restricted to model singularity types, this pseudo-metric is a bona fide metric.
elements.sent-2411-003: In case of the projective space, there is a known one-to-one correspondence between toric model singularity types and convex bodies inside the unit simplex.
elements.sent-2411-004: Hence in this case it is natural to compare the $d_\mathcal S$ metric to the classical Hausdorff metric.
elements.sent-2411-005: We provide precise H\"older bounds, showing that their induced topologies are the same.
elements.sent-2411-006: More generally, we introduce a quasi-metric $d_G$ on the space of compact convex sets inside an arbitrary convex body $G$, with $d_\mathcal S = d_G$ in case $G$ is the unit simplex.
elements.sent-2411-007: We prove optimal H\"older bounds comparing $d_G$ with the Hausdorff metric.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our analysis shows that the H\"older exponents differ depending on the geometry of $G$, with the worst exponents in case $G$ is a polytope, and the best in case $G$ has $C^2$ boundary.
elements.sent-2411-009: The interactions between complex and convex geometry are profound and numerous.
elements.sent-2411-010: See \cite{lazerzfeldmustataconvexandcomplex , \cite{lehmannjiancorrespondencebetweencomplexandconvex , \cite{Nazarovmahlerusingbergmankernel for a few more recent instances of this, along with the recent surveys \cite{bosurvey, yanirsurvey and references therein.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In the presence of toric symmetries, complex geometric phenomenon can often be reduced to studying an analogous problem in convex analysis (see \cite{Fultonsbookontoricvarieties ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: This note follows this tradition as we will analyze certain metrics introduced by Darvas--Di Nezza--Lu in complex geometry in the toric context.
elements.sent-2411-013: Let $(X,\om)$ be a compact K\"ahler manifold.
elements.sent-2411-014: For the basics of pluripotential theory on compact K\"ahler manifolds we refer to the excellent textbook by Guedj--Zeriahi \cite{GZbook .}
elements.sent-2411-015: With slight abuse of precision, an upper semicontinuous function $u : X \to \R\cup \{-\infty\}$ is $\om$-plurisubharmonic ($\om$-psh) if $\om_{u := \om + i\dd\dbar u\geq 0$ in the sense of currents.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We denote by $\PSH(X,\om)$ the set of all $\om$-plurisubharmonic functions.
elements.sent-2411-017: If $u, v \in \PSH(X,\om)$, we say $u$ is more singular than $v$, denoted by $u \preceq v$, if there exists a constant $C$ such that $u \leq v + C$.
elements.sent-2411-018: We say $u$ and $v$ have the same singularity type, denoted by $u \simeq v$, if $u\preceq v$ and $v \preceq u$.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $[u]$ denote the equivalence class of the singularity type of $u$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Not all singularity types are created equal.
elements.sent-2411-021: To solve the complex Monge--Amp\`ere equation with prescribed singularities, in \cite{Darvasmonotonicity Darvas--Di Nezza--Lu introduced the so called model potentials (see \cite{darvas2023relative for a detailed exposition).}}
elements.sent-2411-022: These are the potentials $\phi \in \PSH(X,\om)$ that satisfy \begin{equation}\phi = (\sup\{ u \in \PSH(X,\om) \, : \, u \preceq \phi, u \leq 0\})^{*}\end{equation} where $f^{* (x) = \limsup_{y \to x f(y)$ is the upper semicontinuous regularization of $f$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Slightly deviating from the literature, we denote by $\cS(X,\om)$ the set of all singularity types of model potentials $\phi \in \PSH(X,\om)$ that have positive non-pluripolar mass ($\int_X \omega^n_\phi>0$).
elements.sent-2411-024: For a detailed study of model potentials, we refer to the recent survey \cite{darvas2023relative .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In \cite{darvas2019metric , Darvas--Di Nezza--Lu defined a metric $d_{\cS}$ on $\cS(X,\om)$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The description of $d_{\mathcal{S }$ involves an embedding into the space of weak Mabuchi geodesic rays, and it is quite involved.}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, there is a quasi-metric equivalent to $d_{\mathcal{S }$ which can be described in terms of non-pluripolar products of potentials.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Indeed, in \cite[Proposition 3.5]{darvas2019metric , the authors prove that if $[u], [v] \in \cS(X,\om)$, then \begin{equation}\label{eq: interpreting dS with non-pluripolar volume} d_{\mathcal{S}}([u], [v]) \leq \sum_{j=0}^{n} \left( 2\int_{X} \om^{j}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{\max(u,v)} - \int_{X} \om^{j}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{u} - \int_{X} \om^{j} \wedge \om^{n-j}_{v} \right) \leq C d_{\mathcal{S}}([u],[v]).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq: interpreting dS with non-pluripolar volume} d_{S}([u], [v]) \leq \sum_{j=0}^{n} \left( 2\int_{X} \om^{j}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{\max(u,v)} - \int_{X} \om^{j}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{u} - \int_{X} \om^{j} \wedge \om^{n-j}_{v} \right) \leq C d_{S}([u],[v]).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-029: Here $C > 0$ is a constant that depends only on $n$.
elements.sent-2411-030: For the sake of our note, it is enough to consider the particular case of $X=\CP^{n}$ with the Fubini--Study metric $\omega=\om_{FS}$.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the coordinates $(z_{1 , \dots, z_{n )$, for the embedding of $\C^{n}$ into $\CP^{n}$ (via the map $(z_1,\ldots,z_n) \to [1,z_1,\ldots,z_n]$), the Fubini--Study metric is given by \begin{equation}\om_{FS} = \frac{\sqrt{-1}}{2}\sum_{j,k=1}^{n}\frac{\dd^{2}}{\dd z_{j} \dd \bar{z}_{k}} \ln (1 + |z|^{2})dz_{j} \wedge d\bar{z}_{k}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The potential $\ln(1+ |z|^{2 )$ is a toric symmetric psh function and its corresponding convex function on $\mathbb{R ^n$ is $K(x) := \ln(1+e^{2x_{1 + \dots + e^{2x_{n )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The closure of the subgradient set $\overline{\dd K(\R^{n ) = \De$ is the unit simplex in $\R^{n}$ given by the convex combination of $0, e_{1 , \dots, e_{n}$, where $e_{i = (0, \dots, 1 ,\dots, 0)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: As we now elaborate, due to \cite[Theorem 7.2]{darvaslogconcavity , there is a correspondence between the closed convex subsets of $\De$ and model singularity types with toric symmetries of $\om_{FS}$-psh functions.}
elements.sent-2411-035: If $P \subset \De$ is a closed convex set, let $h_{P : \R^{n \to \R$ be the support function given by \begin{equation}h_{P}(x) = \sup_{p \in P} \langle p, x\rangle.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The corresponding psh function on $(\C^{* )^{n}$ is given by \begin{equation}H_{P}(z) = h_{P}(\log|z_{1}|, \dots, \log|z_{n}|).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The function \begin{equation}\phi_{P}(z) = H_{P}(z) - \frac{1}{2} \ln(1+ |z|^{2})\end{equation} defined on $(\C^{* )^{n}$ extends to all of $\CP^{n}$ as an $\om_{FS}$-psh function.}
elements.sent-2411-038: As pointed out in \cite[Theorem 7.2]{darvaslogconcavity , $\phi_{P}$ has model singularities.}
elements.sent-2411-039: On the other hand, if $\phi \in \PSH(X,\om_{FS )$ has model singularities, and is toric invariant, then we can find a convex function $h$, such that $\phi(z) + \frac{1 {2 \ln (1+ |z|^{2 ) = h(\log|z_{1 |, \dots, \log|z_{n |)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Then $P = \overline{\dd h(\R^{n )}$ is the set of all subdifferentials of $h$ and $\phi \simeq \phi_{P}$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We refer to \cite[Section 7]{darvaslogconcavity for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The expression in the middle of \eqref{eq: interpreting dS with non-pluripolar volume has an interpretation in terms of convex geometry using mixed volumes of convex bodies.}
elements.sent-2411-045: As explained under \cite[equation (37)]{darvaslogconcavity and \cite{Bayraktarzerodistribution , the non-pluripolar volume can be interpreted using the classical mixed volumes $MV_j(\cdot,\cdot)$ of convex geometry (see Definition \ref{mixed_vol_def ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: We observe that $\max\{h_{K , h_{L}\} = h_{K\hull L}$ and consequently $\max\{\phi_{K , \phi_{L}\} = \phi_{K\hull L}$ where $K\hull L$ is the convex hull of $K \cup L$.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: As a result, the expression in the middle of\eqref{eq: interpreting dS with non-pluripolar volume with $u$ and $v$ replaced by $\phi_{K}$ and $\phi_{L}$ is equal to \begin{equation}\label{eq: equation for quasi-metrics} d_{\De}(K,L) : = d_\mathcal S(\phi_K, \phi_L)= \sum_{j=0}^{n}\left( 2\MV_{j}(\De, K\hull L) - \MV_{j}(\De, K) - \MV_{j}(\De, L) \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq: equation for quasi-metrics} d_{\De}(K,L) : = d_\mathcal S(\phi_K, \phi_L)= \sum_{j=0}^{n}\left( 2\MV_{j}(\De, K\hull L) - \MV_{j}(\De, K) - \MV_{j}(\De, L) \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: Here $\MV_{j (\De, K) = \MV(\De[n-j], K[j])$ is the mixed volume of $\De$ and $K$ with $K$ appearing $j$ times.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Thus~\eqref{eq: equation for quasi-metrics induces a quasi-metric on $\mathcal{K (\De)$, the space of all compact convex subsets of $\De$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: A quasi-metric induces a metrizable topology on $\mathcal{K (\De)$ (see \cite{Paluszynski2009OnQA ).}}
elements.sent-2411-051: It raises a natural question: is this metrizable topology the same as the one induced by the familiar Hausdorff metric $d_{\H}$? In this paper, we answer this question affirmatively.
elements.sent-2411-052: More precisely, we prove the following result that holds more generally for closed convex subsets of a fixed convex body $G$:.
elements.sent-2411-053: Let $G \subset \R^{n}$ be a convex body (compact convex set with non-empty interior).
elements.sent-2411-054: Let $\mathcal{K (G)$ denote the set of all compact convex subsets of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We consider the following expression for $K,L \in \mathcal K(G)$: \begin{equation}\label{eq: expression for quasi-metric for arbitrary convex body} d_{G} (K, L) := \sum_{j=0}^{n}\left( 2\MV_{j}(G, K\hull L) - \MV_{j}(G, K) - \MV_{j}(G, L) \right)\end{equation} There exists $C > 1$ that depends only on $G$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq: bound for general reference body} \frac{1}{C}d_{\H}(K,L)^n \leq d_{G}(K,L) \leq Cd_{\H}(K,L), \ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq: expression for quasi-metric for arbitrary convex body} d_{G} (K, L) := \sum_{j=0}^{n}\left( 2\MV_{j}(G, K\hull L) - \MV_{j}(G, K) - \MV_{j}(G, L) \right)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\label{eq: bound for general reference body} \frac{1}{C}d_{\H}(K,L)^n \leq d_{G}(K,L) \leq Cd_{\H}(K,L), \ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: In the case $G = \Delta$, the estimates of \eqref{eq: bound for general reference body imply that $(\mathcal K(\Delta),d_\Delta)$ is complete, because so is $(\mathcal K(\Delta),d_\mathcal H)$, due to the Blashke selection lemma.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Remarkably, the analogous result in the K\"ahler case for general singularity types only holds under the assumption of non-vanishing mass, as pointed out in \cite[Theorem 4.9]{darvas2019metric and the example in Section 4.2 of the same paper.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Abusing precision, in this note we will refer to $d_{G}$ as a quasi-metric (satisfying only the quasi-triangle inequality).
elements.sent-2411-059: We will never assume that the quasi-triangle inequality for $d_G$ holds since this is only known in case $G = \De$.
elements.sent-2411-060: However in the context of the relative pluripotential theory described in \cite{darvas2023relative , there is a natural avenue to obtain the quasi-triangle inequality for general $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, one can introduce a `relative metric' $d_{\mathcal{S , \phi}$ on the space of relative singularity types $\mathcal{S (X,\om, \phi) = \left\{[u] : u \preceq \phi, u \text{ is a model potential }\right\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: It is natural to expect that \begin{equation}d_{S,\phi}([u], [v]) \leq \sum_{j=0}^{n}2\int_{X} \om^{j}_{\phi}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{\max\{u,v\}} - \int_{X} \om^{j}_{\phi}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{u} - \int_{X} \om^{j}_{\phi}\wedge \om^{n-j}_{v} \leq d_{\cS, \phi}([u], [v]).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: In the particular case when $\phi$ is equal to $\phi_{G}$, the potential induced by the convex body $G$, we would obtain that $d_{G}$ as described in~\eqref{eq: expression for quasi-metric for arbitrary convex body is indeed a quasi-metric, just like $d_{\De}$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: When the reference convex body $G$ has $C^{2}$ boundary, the H\"older estimates of the previous result can be sharpened:.
elements.sent-2411-065: If $G \subset \R^{n}$ is a compact subset of $\R^{n}$ with non-empty interior and $C^2$ boundary, then there exists $C > 1$ depending only on $G$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq: bound for smooth body} \frac{1}{C}d_{\H} (K, L)^{\frac{n+1}{2}} \leq d_{G} (K,L) \leq C d_{\H}(K,L), \ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{eq: bound for smooth body} \frac{1}{C}d_{\H} (K, L)^{\frac{n+1}{2}} \leq d_{G} (K,L) \leq C d_{\H}(K,L), \ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: Lastly, we argue that the H\"older estimates in the previous theorems are in a certain sense optimal:.
elements.sent-2411-067: Let $G \subset \R^{n}$ be a compact subset of $\R^{n}$ with non-empty interior.
elements.sent-2411-068: For any $\bb > 1$, there is no $C > 0$ such that \begin{equation}d_{G}(K,L) \leq C d_{\H}(K,L)^{\bb}, \ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: For any $p < \frac{n+1 {2}$ there is no $C$ such that \begin{equation}Cd_{\H}(K,L)^{p} \leq d_{G}(K,L)\ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Lastly, if $G$ is a convex polytope, then for any $p < n$, there is no $C> 0$ such that \begin{equation}C d_{\H}(K,L)^{p} \leq d_{G}(K,L)\ \ K,L \in \mathcal K(G).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-071: With regards to future directions, it would be intriguing to prove that $d_G$ satisfies a quasi-triangle inequality using only convex analysis, without any reference to complex geometry (see Remark \ref{rem: quasi-trianle_ineq ).}
elements.sent-2411-072: We expect that our analysis carries through for other types of toric K\"ahler manifolds as well, not just complex projective space.
elements.sent-2411-073: Still, there is interest in seeing if any difficulties arise, and we leave these to the interested reader.
elements.sent-2411-074: In Section~\ref{sec: Background , we explain our notations and recall relevant results from the literature.}
elements.sent-2411-075: In Section~\ref{sec: holder estimates we prove Theorems~\ref{thm: Main theorem and~\ref{thm: improvement for smooth bodies .}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: In Section~\ref{sec: holder exponent optimality , we prove Theorem~\ref{thm: bounds are optimal .}}

elements.sent-2411-084: In this section, we introduce notation and gather some relevant definitions and theorems.
elements.sent-2411-086: We also consider some more specific notation.
elements.sent-2411-087: For two points $p, q \in \R^n$, let \begin{equation}[p,q] := \{p\} \,\hull\, \{q\} \quad and \quad [p,q) := [p,q]\setminus q.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-088: By $\mathcal K^n$ we mean the space of compact, convex sets in $\R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-089: If $G \in \K^n$ has nonempty interior, we call it a convex body.
elements.sent-2411-090: Given convex body $G$, we define $\K(G)$ to be the space of compact, convex subsets of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Furthermore, $B$ is the closed unit ball centered at the origin and $\omega_k$ is the volume of the unit $k$-ball in $\R^k$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Let $K, L$ be two compact subsets of $\R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Hausdorff distance between them is \begin{equation}d_\H(K,L)=\inf\{\delta > 0 : K \subset L + \delta B, \; L \subset K + \delta B\},\end{equation} or equivalently \begin{equation}d_\H(K,L) = \max \left\{\sup_{x \in K} d(x, L), \sup_{y \in L} d(y, K)\right\},\end{equation} where $d(x,L) := \min\{|x - y| : y \in L\}.$.
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}d_\H(K,L)=\inf\{\delta > 0 : K \subset L + \delta B, \; L \subset K + \delta B\},\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}d_\H(K,L) = \max \left\{\sup_{x \in K} d(x, L), \sup_{y \in L} d(y, K)\right\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: By the Blaschke selection theorem, the Hausdorff distance turns $\mathcal K^n$ into a complete metric space with the Heine-Borel property (\cite{schneider2014 , Theorem 1.8.7).}
elements.sent-2411-095: We next recall the definition of the support function of a convex set.
elements.sent-2411-096: Given $K \in \mathcal K^n$, its support function $h_K : \R^n \to \R$ is \begin{equation}h_K(x) = \max\{u \cdot x : u \in K\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-097: Here, $u\cdot x$ denotes the Euclidean inner product between $u$ and $x$.
elements.sent-2411-098: The support $h_K$ is continuous and uniquely describes $K$.
elements.sent-2411-099: For other elementary properties of the support function, we refer the reader to Chapter 2.3 of \cite{hug2020lectures .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, we recall the definition of the mixed volume.
elements.sent-2411-101: The real number $\MV(K_1,\dots,K_n)$ is called the mixed volume of $K_1,\dots,K_n$.
elements.sent-2411-102: We use the following known properties of mixed volumes.
elements.sent-2411-103: If $Q_1,\dots,Q_n \in \mathcal K^n$ are such that $K_j \subseteq Q_j$ for each $j$, then \begin{equation}\MV(K_1,\dots,K_n) \leq \MV(Q_1,\dots,Q_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-105: If $j \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ and $Q_j \in \mathcal K^n$, then \begin{equation}\MV(K_1,\dots, K_j + Q_j, \dots, K_n) = \MV(K_1,\dots, K_n) + \MV(K_1,\dots,Q_j,\dots,K_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-108: We will make use of the following alternative notations for mixed volume.
elements.sent-2411-109: If $m \geq 1$ and $K_1,\dots,K_m \in \mathcal K^n$, we will write \begin{equation}\MV(K_1[\alpha_1], \dots, K_m[\alpha_m]) := \MV(\underbrace{K_1,\dots,K_1}_{\alpha_1 times}, \dots, \underbrace{K_m,\dots,K_m}_{\alpha_m times}),\end{equation} for non-negative integers $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_m$ with $\alpha_1 + \cdots + \alpha_m = n$.
elements.sent-2411-110: As mentioned in Section ~\ref{sec: Introduction , if $K,L \in \mathcal K^n$, we will simply write \begin{equation}\label{eq: mixed_volume} \MV_j(K,L) := \MV(K[n - j], L[j]).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-111: We recall and formally define $d_G$ as presented in Theorem \ref{thm: Main theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-112: For a convex body $G \subset \R^n$, let $d_G: \mathcal{K (G) \times \mathcal{K (G) \to \R$ be the quasi-metric \begin{equation}\label{eqn: dg-def} d_G(K,L) := \sum_{j=1}^n\left(2\MV_j(G, K\hull L)-\MV_j(G,K)-\MV_j(G,L)\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: We define an alternative, related quasi-metric on $\mathcal K(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-114: Letting $G$, $K$, and $L$ be as in Definition \ref{def: quasi-metric-defn , let \begin{equation}\label{eqn: rhoG-def} \rho_G(K,L) := 2\V(G+K\hull L)-\V(G+K)-\V(G+L)\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-115: That $\rho_G$ is a quasi-metric follows from the fact that $\rho_G$ and $d_G$ are quasi-isometric.. More specifically, \begin{equation}\label{eqn: rhoG-dG-qisometry} d_G(K,L) \leq \rho_G(K,L) \leq \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} d_G(K,L).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-116: Indeed, by Equation \eqref{eq: minkow-sum-poly-mv , it follows \begin{equation}\rho_G(K,L) = \sum_{j=1}^n\binom{n}{j}\left(2\MV_j(G,K\hull L)-\MV_j(G,K)-\MV_j(G,L)\right) \geq \sum_{j=1}^n 2\MV_j(G,K\hull L)-\MV_j(G,K)-\MV_j(G,L) =d_G(K,L).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Similarly, \begin{equation}\rho_G(K,L) = \sum_{j=1}^n \binom{n}{j} \left(2\MV_j(G,K\hull L)-\MV_j(G,K)-\MV_j(G,L)\right) \leq \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor} \sum_{j=1}^n 2\MV_j(G,K\hull L)-\MV_j(G,K)-\MV_j(G,L) = \binom{n}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}d_G(K,L),\end{equation} which yields Equation \ref{eqn: rhoG-dG-qisometry .}
elements.section-2411-003: H\"older Estimates for General Bodies and Smooth Bodies
elements.sent-2411-118: In this section, we prove \ref{thm: Main theorem .}
elements.sent-2411-119: To do this, we prove Theorem \ref{thm: lipschitz-upper and Theorem \ref{thm: general-holder-lower , which, when combined, gives Theorem \ref{thm: Main theorem .}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The addition of Theorem \ref{thm: c2-holder-lower gives the refinement of Theorem \ref{thm: improvement for smooth bodies .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: There exists $C > 0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eqn: Lipschitz-Upper} d_G(K,L) \leq C \cdot d_\H(K,L)\end{equation} for all $K,L \in \mathcal K(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Using the linearity and monotonicity of mixed volume (Theorem \ref{thm: mv-properties (2,4,5)) with the definition of Hausdorff distance (\ref{def: hausdorff-dist ) shows that for sets $U,V,K_1,\dots,K_{n-1 \in \mathcal K(G)$, \begin{equation}|\MV(K_1,\dots,K_{n-1}, U) - \MV(K_1,\dots,K_{n-1},V)| \leq \MV(K_1,\dots,K_{n-1},B)d_\H(U,V).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Therefore, since $K,L, K\hull L \subset G$, we have \begin{equation}\MV_j(G, K\hull L) - \MV_j(G, K) =\sum_{\ell=0}^{j-1} \MV(G[n-j], K\hull L[\ell + 1], K[j - \ell - 1]) - \MV(G[n-j], K\hull L[\ell], K[j - \ell]) \leq \sum_{\ell=0}^{j-1} \MV_1(G, B)d_\H(K\hull L, K) \leq j\MV_1(G, B)d_\H(K,L).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}\MV_j(G, K\hull L) - \MV_j(G, K) =\sum_{\ell=0}^{j-1} \MV(G[n-j], K\hull L[\ell + 1], K[j - \ell - 1]) - \MV(G[n-j], K\hull L[\ell], K[j - \ell]) \leq \sum_{\ell=0}^{j-1} \MV_1(G, B)d_\H(K\hull L, K) \leq j\MV_1(G, B)d_\H(K,L).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-124: The final inequality comes from the fact $d_\H(K\hull L, K) \leq d_\H(K,L)$.

elements.sent-2411-251: The set on the right hand side is a spherical cap of radius $r$ and height $t$.
elements.sent-2411-252: The volume of such a cap is, by Lemma \ref{lemma: cap-vol-lemma , \begin{equation}\V(C_{rB}(u, t)) =\omega_{n-1}r^n\int_0^{\arccos(1-t/r)}(\sin\theta)^n\,\mathrm{d}\theta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-253: From the estimates $\sin\theta\leq\theta$ and $\arccos(1-x)\leq\pi\sqrt{x/2}$ for $0\leq x\leq1$, we obtain \begin{equation}d_G(C_t,S_t) \leq2^n\V(C_t)\leq\omega_{n-1}r^n\int_0^{\pi\sqrt{t/2r}}\theta^n\,d\theta=\frac{\omega_{n-1}\pi^{n+1}r^n}{n+1}\left(\frac{t}{2r}\right)^{\frac{n+1}{2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-254: By (1) in Lemma \ref{lemm: cap-counterexample , it follows \begin{equation}\label{eqn: n+1/2 upper} d_G(C_t, S_t) \leq C'd_\H(C_t, S_t)^{\frac{n+1}{2}}\end{equation} where $C' = \frac{\omega_{n-1}\pi^{n+1 {n+1 (2r)^{\frac{n-1 {2 > 0$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-084: \begin{equation}\label{eqn: n+1/2 upper} d_G(C_t, S_t) \leq C'd_\H(C_t, S_t)^{\frac{n+1}{2}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-255: Combining (2) of Lemma \ref{lemm: cap-counterexample with Equation \ref{eqn: n+1/2 upper , we deduce that the inequality \begin{equation}C\cdot d_\H(K,L)^p\leq d_G(K,L)\end{equation} does not hold for for all $K$ and $L$ for any choice of $C > 0$ when $p<\frac{n+1 {2}$.}}}
